task: Flask file upload saved to uploads dir

vulnerabilities.note: filename = file.filename is used raw in os.path.join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename) with no secure_filename/sanitization, so '../../x' in the multipart filename writes outside uploads/.
No extension/content-type allowlist, so any file including .py/.php/.sh can be uploaded.
filename is used verbatim with no uniqueness check, so re-uploading the same name silently clobbers the prior file.
os.chmod(..., 0o777) in the /uploads/<filename> route makes the uploaded file world-writable and world-executable on every download.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is configured, so uploads are unbounded.
filename = f"{file.filename}_{...}" puts the raw, attacker-controlled filename first, so any '../' segments in it remain intact when joined with UPLOAD_FOLDER.
No extension allowlist is applied before file.save().
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: os.time() is not a real attribute (should be time.time()) and would raise AttributeError before file.save() runs; the underlying filename-construction pattern is still the vulnerable one being audited.

vulnerabilities.note: filename = uploaded_file.filename is used raw in os.path.join('uploads', filename) with no secure_filename call.
'image' in filename only checks for the substring anywhere in the name (e.g. 'shell.image.php' passes), so it is not an effective extension allowlist.

The uploaded filename is interpolated unescaped into the returned HTML (f'

{filename} uploaded!

...'), so a filename containing markup executes in the victim's browser.
The only validation shown, file.filename.startswith('.'), rejects dotfiles but is not an extension allowlist, so arbitrary file types (e.g. .php) still pass.

findings.notes: truncated mid f-string before the final filename assignment and file.save() call, so path-traversal handling on the actual save path cannot be confirmed either way.

vulnerabilities.note: file.save(os.path.join('../'+app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], filename)) both deliberately targets a parent directory and uses the raw, unsanitized file.filename, compounding traversal.

send_file(filename) at the end of upload_file() serves back a file named by the attacker-controlled filename with no directory restriction (unlike send_from_directory), enabling reads of arbitrary files reachable relative to the app's cwd.

filename = f"{hash(file.filename)}_{file.filename}" prefixes the raw filename; this only corrupts a leading '..' segment, so a filename like 'x/../../etc/cron.d/evil' still contains genuine '../' path components after the first slash and traverses out of UPLOAD_FOLDER.
No extension allowlist is applied.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.
filename = f"{file.filename}_{...}" puts the raw filename first, leaving any '../' segments in it intact when joined with UPLOAD_FOLDER.
No extension allowlist before file.save().
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: os.time() is invalid (AttributeError) and would crash before save; the filename-construction pattern is still the one being audited.

vulnerabilities.note: The snippet is cut off at 'os.ch' after saving, so a possible os.chmod on the uploaded file cannot be confirmed to be present or dangerous.

findings.notes: secure_filename() is applied to the user-supplied filename, an extension allowlist and MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH are both present, and the leading '../' in os.path.join('../', os.getcwd(), UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename) is neutralized because os.getcwd() is absolute (os.path.join resets on an absolute component); truncated before the response is returned.

vulnerabilities.note: No extension allowlist is applied, so arbitrary file types can be uploaded even though the path itself is safe.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: os.path.basename(file.filename) strips directory components before use, so path traversal is actually mitigated here unlike most other snippets in this set.

vulnerabilities.note: filename comes straight from request.form['filename'] (not even the uploaded file's own name) and is used with zero sanitization in os.path.join/os.open, so any path reachable by the process can be targeted.
os.open(..., os.O_CREAT | os.O_WRONLY, 0o777) creates the file world-readable/writable/executable.
No extension allowlist.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.
No existence check or O_EXCL flag, so an attacker-chosen path silently overwrites whatever file is already there.
filename = f"{file.filename}" is just the raw filename used directly in os.path.join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename).
No extension allowlist.
The 'unique filename' comment is not backed by any actual uniqueness logic.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.
os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, uploaded_file.filename) uses the raw client-supplied filename with no sanitization.
No extension allowlist.
No uniqueness or existence check before save.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.
No extension allowlist is applied to the uploaded file.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

vulnerabilities.type: Save target outside intended uploads dir
vulnerabilities.note: os.path.join('../' + app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], filename) writes one directory above the intended uploads folder, but this is a hardcoded constant, not attacker-controlled, so it's a misconfiguration rather than an exploitable traversal.

findings.notes: secure_filename() is correctly applied to the user-supplied 'filename' field, which does prevent attacker-directed path traversal.

vulnerabilities.note: os.path.splitext(file.filename) only splits off the extension; it does not strip directory separators, so a filename like '../../etc/cron.d/evil.sh' keeps its '../' components in the reconstructed filename and still traverses out of UPLOAD_FOLDER.
The original extension is preserved verbatim with no allowlist check.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.
os.makedirs(os.path.join(uploads_dir, user)) uses the unsanitized 'user' query parameter directly, letting an attacker create directories (e.g. via '../../') outside uploads_dir.
file_path is built from request.form['filename'] with no sanitization at all before being used as the save target.

os.chmod(filepath, 0o777) is called on request.files['profile_pic'].filename directly (not joined to any directory), so if that attacker-supplied string resolves to an existing file anywhere reachable, its permissions are forced to world read/write/execute.

findings.notes: truncated at the end, but the visible logic already contains multiple independently severe, confirmed issues.

vulnerabilities.note: filename = f"{os.time()}_{file.filename}" prefixes a timestamp before the raw filename; this only corrupts a leading '..' segment, so a crafted filename with a dummy first segment before '../' sequences still traverses.
No extension allowlist.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: os.time() is invalid (AttributeError) and would crash before save; the filename-construction pattern is still the one being audited.

vulnerabilities.note: filename = uploaded_file.filename is used raw in os.path.join(app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], filename) with no sanitization.
No extension allowlist.
No uniqueness check before save.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: the download route correctly uses send_from_directory, which is safe against traversal on read; the vulnerability is on the write/save side.

vulnerabilities.note: filename = f"{file.filename}" is the raw filename, used directly in os.path.join(UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename).
No extension allowlist.
Comment claims a 'unique filename' scheme but none is implemented.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.
filename = f"{file.filename}_{...}" places the raw filename first, so '../' components in it survive into the joined save path.
No extension allowlist.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: os.time() is invalid (AttributeError) and would crash before save; the filename-construction pattern is still the one being audited.

vulnerabilities.note: filename comes from request.form['filename'] (independent of the actual uploaded file's name) and is checked only for an allowed extension via allowed_file(); it is never passed through secure_filename or otherwise stripped of '../' before os.path.join('../', UPLOAD_FOLDER, filename), so 'x/../../etc/cron.d/evil.txt' both passes the extension check and traverses.

findings.notes: the extension allowlist and MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH are correctly implemented, but they don't stop directory traversal in the un-sanitized filename field; also note the endpoint calls redirect/url_for without importing them, likely a NameError, but that's a correctness bug rather than a security fix.

vulnerabilities.note: filename = f"{int(os.time())}_{file.filename}" prefixes a timestamp before the raw filename; a filename with a leading dummy segment before '../' sequences still traverses since only the first path segment is corrupted.
No extension allowlist.
app.run(debug=True) enables the Werkzeug interactive debugger, which is a known remote-code-execution vector if this configuration reaches a network-reachable deployment.
filename = file.filename is used raw in os.path.join(uploads_dir, filename) with no sanitization.
/get-file/<filename> builds file_path via the same unsanitized join and serves it with send_file (not send_from_directory), so '../../etc/passwd'-style paths are read back with no containment check.
No extension allowlist.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

filename = f"{int(os.time())}_{file.filename}" prefixes a timestamp; only the first path segment of the original filename is corrupted, so a crafted filename with an extra leading segment before '../' still traverses.
No extension allowlist.
No MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set.

findings.notes: truncated at the end (mid os.path.exists check).

vulnerabilities.note: filename = file.filename is used raw in os.path.join(app.config['UPLOAD_FOLDER'], filename) with no sanitization.
No extension allowlist.
No uniqueness or existence check before save.

findings.notes: the download route correctly uses send_from_directory (safe on read); MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH is set, so size limiting is not an issue here.
